text: The task-positive network — a loosely allied set of regions including the dorsolateral prefrontal cortex, anterior insula, and intraparietal sulcus — activates when we engage in goal-directed cognition. What makes it remarkable is its relationship to the default mode network: the two systems are anti-correlated. As one rises, the other falls. This competitive dynamic means that focus is not just the activation of attention circuits but the suppression of the self-referential ones. Distraction, in this light, is not merely the presence of interference — it is the failure of the task-positive network to dominate its rival, allowing the interior monologue to flood back in.
question: What does the anti-correlation between the task-positive and default mode networks mean for distraction?

options.text: Distraction is caused by insufficient oxygen to the prefrontal cortex
Distraction occurs when both networks activate simultaneously
Distraction is the failure of the task-positive network to suppress the default mode network
Distraction results from overstimulation of the anterior insula
